fields.definition_origin: John Sweller (UNSW), late 1980s — codified in Sweller (1988) 'Cognitive load during problem solving'. Distinguishes intrinsic load (the actual task complexity), extraneous load (interface noise, redundant labels, decorative chrome), and germane load (effort spent constructing useful mental schemas). Working-memory capacity ~4 ± 1 chunks (Cowan 2001). Applied to onboarding via NN/g and the PMC11422584 (2024) mobile-learning cognitive-load questionnaire validation.
fields.core_mechanism: Working memory is a strict bottleneck — anything beyond ~4 chunks leaks. Onboarding screens that surface multiple choices, multiple actions, or multiple unfamiliar concepts simultaneously force users to context-switch in working memory, raising extraneous load and accelerating abandonment. Each screen must do exactly one thing: ask exactly one question, show exactly one CTA, or surface exactly one concept. The PMC12956955 anchor study extends this: initial complexity perception in the first ~7-10 seconds is consolidated as a persistent anchor that overrides actual app utility — meaning a high-load opening screen damages the entire app's perceived value, not just that screen's completion rate. Implication: load minimization in onboarding is not a UX nicety, it's a perceived-value lever.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Working memory capacity in CLT is linked to dorsolateral PFC and parietal cortex activity (Baddeley's working-memory model maps to prefrontal-parietal circuits). Extraneous load produces measurable pupil dilation (Beatty 1982) and increased anterior cingulate cortex engagement on choice difficulty (Wu et al. 2018, PMC5998988). Schema construction (germane load) recruits hippocampus for binding and consolidation.
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Sweller (1988) original CLT formulation, meta-validated across decades; instrument validation (Frontiers in Psychology 2017) confirms intrinsic/extraneous/germane separability. (2) PMC11422584 (2024) — mobile-learning cognitive-load questionnaire validation enables item-by-item load measurement on individual onboarding screens (sample N>400). (3) PMC12956955 anchor study (N1=100, N2=87, N3=80) — initial complexity perception persists regardless of subsequent utility; effect significant across three replications. (4) Material 3 Expressive (I/O 2025, 18,000 participants, 46 studies) — expressive yet well-grouped UIs achieve 4x faster element recognition vs baseline M3, operationalizing load reduction at scale. (5) NN/g eye-tracking confirms grouped elements scanned in fewer fixations. (6) Stripe Checkout's three-field minimal-load form is industry-cited as conversion-best in class.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Step-level abandonment rate, time-on-step distribution (long tail = overload), NASA-TLX subjective load score per step, eye-tracking fixation count, error rate on per-step input, pupil dilation in usability lab, post-onboarding NPS, perceived-complexity Likert at first session.
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Material 3 Expressive 18k-participant study: up to 4x faster element recognition (eye-tracking) on load-reduced expressive UIs vs baseline. PMC12956955: initial complexity perception persists across 3 replications and predicts long-term utility judgment. Stripe Checkout (cited industry data): three-field minimal-load checkout cited as conversion-best in class (~10-25% lift over multi-field competitors). One-question-per-screen conversational onboarding (Cal AI, Headway, Flo) outperforms multi-question form-style by 30-50% completion in published teardowns.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) One-thing-per-screen rule: each onboarding screen asks exactly one question or shows exactly one concept; if two concepts must coexist, split the screen. (2) Cap visible choices: Hick's-Law-aligned, ≤5 options per screen, ≤4 for the conversational big-button pattern. (3) Progressive disclosure: defer secondary settings to post-onboarding (NN/g canonical pattern). (4) Strip extraneous load: kill decorative dividers, redundant labels, multi-color icon sets in onboarding; whitespace at 8/16/24/32dp scale instead of rules. (5) Use chunking + endowed progress for multi-step flows ('Step 2 of 8' starting at 2). (6) Skeleton screens trigger closure perception, lowering perceived load. (7) Save-as-you-go to free working memory from 'will this be saved?' tax. (8) Pin primary action in thumb zone — physical effort is part of Fogg's Ability calculus and adds to load.
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cal AI's 33-step onboarding — paradoxically high *step count* but minimal *per-step load*: each screen asks one question with 3-4 big-button options, conversational framing. The compound investment effect compensates for length. (2) Headway's reading-app onboarding — single concept per screen (genre, time-per-day, topic interest), each step a 4-option big-button picker. (3) Stripe Checkout — three fields visible, no logo, no nav, no extraneous load — cited as conversion-best in class. (4) iOS Settings (iOS 17+) — categorized into ~12 top groups vs iOS 6's flat 30+ list (consolidation by progressive disclosure). (5) Linear empty-state onboarding — single primary action ('Create your first issue'), zero decorative chrome. (6) Anti-example: 'customize every setting' onboarding screens with 20 toggles upfront — extraneous load at the worst moment.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile is significantly more load-sensitive: smaller viewport amplifies any clutter, thumb travel adds physical cost, and the user's attention is more divided (in-public, multitasking). Apply per-screen load minimization more aggressively on mobile (≤4 tab-bar items per Apple HIG, ≤5 per Material). Web has more parallel-choice tolerance and command-palette mitigations (Cmd+K) unavailable on mobile. Web equivalent: progressive form fields with inline validation; mobile-primary for one-thing-per-screen discipline.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Strongly aligned with both. Apple HIG explicitly cites 'aesthetic integrity' and progressive disclosure; Liquid Glass (iOS 26+) bolder left-aligned typography + translucent material reduces perceived load. Material 3 Expressive empirically validates that minimalist + expressive can coexist — recognition speed up to 4x faster — provided grouping discipline is intact. Both platforms ship-faster guidance: do not fight the grid system or component density.
fields.category_fit: Universal — every category benefits. Critical for: AI-wrapper (chat-first apps risk overloading first message), wellness/habit (long questionnaires must minimize per-step load), fintech (KYC compliance fields must be chunked across screens). Less critical for: utility (single-feature apps already minimal). DO NOT use multi-question form-style onboarding for any consumer category in 2026 except mandatory KYC.
fields.tooling_options: Plotline (one-screen-per-step builder), Adapty (server-driven onboarding sequencing), OnboardJS, Storyly, Appcues Mobile. For load measurement: PostHog session recordings, UXCam autocapture (heatmaps + drop-off), Hotjar (web). For eye-tracking validation in lab: Tobii, Pupil Labs (research only).
fields.ai_dependency: none: cognitive-load discipline is a static design constraint; no LLM call required. Optional small-model use: server-side classifier to flag onboarding screens with too many visible elements during code review.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to apply explicit numeric caps and one-thing-per-screen discipline. Example fragment 1: 'Build the height-input onboarding screen. Hard caps: one question on screen, ≤5 visible UI elements total (header, question, picker, primary CTA, skip), one corner-radius value, two type weights max. Header at 28pt Cabinet Grotesk, question prompt at 18pt Manrope. Picker is wheel-style with 5 visible rows. CTA pinned bottom thumb-zone, 56dp tall. No decorative dividers, no progress percentage, no inline tooltips — defer all secondary affordances.' Example fragment 2: 'Audit this onboarding screen against Cognitive Load Theory: list every visible interactive element and justify (intrinsic load) or flag for removal (extraneous load). Output the audit as a comment block above the component. Forbid >5 visible interactive elements per onboarding screen.'
fields.stitch_application: Stitch needs explicit one-thing rules because it cannot infer Gestalt. Example fragment 1: 'Mobile onboarding screen 4 of 8, dimensions 390x844. ONE question only: \'How tall are you?\'. Layout (top to bottom): 1) progress bar at top showing 4 of 8 filled (dark indigo, 4dp tall, full-width-minus-32dp-padding), 2) 32dp gap, 3) question in 28pt Cabinet Grotesk Bold left-aligned, 4) 24dp gap, 5) wheel picker (5 rows visible, center row highlighted with brand-color background), 6) flex spacer, 7) bottom CTA Continue in pill button 56dp tall, full-width, brand-color background. NO decorative elements. NO secondary text. NO icons except in the progress bar. Forbid additional questions on this screen.' Example fragment 2: 'For each onboarding screen, output a load-audit table at the top: visible elements count (must be ≤5), interactive elements count (must be ≤2 — primary CTA + skip), text blocks count (must be ≤3 — header, question, helper).'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI defaults to dumping every imaginable feature/choice on a single onboarding screen — name field + email field + age picker + gender picker + 'how did you hear about us' + ToS checkbox, all on the same screen. Also defaults to long flat dropdowns instead of progressive 4-button big-button screens, and adds decorative dividers between every row. Counter-prompt: 'Hard-cap one question per onboarding screen. Convert all dropdowns with >4 options into separate big-button screens. No decorative dividers — use whitespace at the 8/16/32dp scale. Output the visible-element count at the top of each screen file as a comment; reject builds where any onboarding screen exceeds 5 visible interactive elements.'
fields.anti_patterns: Cross-references the Six-Antipattern Audit's 'Wall-of-Text + Cognitive Overload (deck-of-cards carousel)' and 'Customize every setting' Day-0 screens. Specific don'ts: (1) multi-question form-style onboarding (DMV-style); (2) decorative dividers between every list row; (3) tooltips that hide critical info — recall cost > recognition; (4) modals stacked on modals; (5) mixed icon styles in onboarding header; (6) progress percentages with arbitrary numeric precision (87% complete); (7) inline validation that fires before user finishes typing.
fields.regulatory_risk: Low: reducing cognitive load is generally pro-user. Minor risk: aggressive simplification can hide consent or pricing — DSA / GDPR / FTC Click-to-Cancel require informed consent visibility. Do NOT use cognitive-load arguments to bury legal disclosures or auto-renewal terms; this becomes a dark pattern. EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) explicitly flags obstructive disclosures.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple Guideline 5.1.1(v) data minimization in onboarding indirectly supports CLT discipline. Apple HIG progressive disclosure is required in 2026 reviews. Material 3 Expressive recognition-speed metrics inform Play Store quality scoring. WCAG 2.2 §3.3.7 redundant entry — onboarding must not ask the same info twice across steps.
fields.accessibility_implications: Per-screen load reduction directly helps cognitive-impairment users. But aggressive minimalism can hide affordances from screen readers — provide explicit ARIA landmarks and labels even when visual chrome is stripped. Dynamic-type support to 200% required (WCAG 2.2). Avoid icon-only CTAs in onboarding; pair every icon with a label for VoiceOver/TalkBack. Skeleton screens must announce loading state to assistive tech. Sub-pixel layout shift breaks pre-attentive grouping — use consistent rendering.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Over-minimalism — hiding too much breaks discoverability (the 2010s 'hamburger everywhere' era). (2) CLT's intrinsic/germane separation is contested in education research. (3) Skeleton screens too long feel like broken state — cap at 800ms before showing content or fallback. (4) Performance: pre-attentive Gestalt grouping requires consistent rendering — sub-pixel jitter kills the perception. (5) One-thing-per-screen conflicts with development convenience (more screens, more state) — invest in a state-machine architecture (XState) to amortize.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Largely universal: working-memory capacity is biologically grounded. Cultural calibration applies to information density tolerance — Japanese, Chinese, Korean users tolerate higher density (Yahoo Japan, Naver, Tmall) without subjective overload, while Western users prefer more whitespace. Do NOT assume Western minimalism is universally optimal; A/B test density per locale. RTL languages require mirrored grouping but underlying CLT principles hold.
fields.prescribed_action: Enforce one-thing-per-screen in onboarding: every screen asks exactly one question or shows exactly one concept, ≤5 visible interactive elements total, ≤4 big-button options for choice screens. Output a load-audit comment block at the top of each onboarding component listing visible-element count; reject builds where any onboarding screen exceeds the cap.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: Directly determines questionnaire architecture: each question must occupy its own screen (conversational, not form-style); options ≤4 per question for big-button pattern; question copy ≤2 lines; no inline helper text beyond a single sentence. Compounds with sunk-cost effect — many low-load screens beat fewer high-load screens.
fields.aha_relevance: CLT preserves the cognitive bandwidth required to *feel* the Aha moment. A user overloaded by extraneous load on screens 1-29 cannot emotionally register the AI plan reveal at screen 30. Load-discipline is what makes the engineered peak land.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Universal — load discipline applies to every archetype. Especially aligned with Sage (clarity, no clutter), Magician (concentrated revelation), Caregiver (gentle, no pressure). Conflicts slightly with Maximalist Jester aesthetics that rely on visual density — but even maximalist must respect per-screen action load.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Wall-of-Text + Cognitive Overload (deck-of-cards carousel); 'Customize every setting' Day-0; Decision Paralysis (5+ plans on paywall — Hick's Law violation).
sources: [Element Interactivity and Intrinsic, Extraneous, Germane Load — Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-010-9128-5)
[Mobile Learning Cognitive Load Questionnaire Validation — PMC11422584 (2024)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11422584/)
[Hick-Hyman Law mediated by cognitive control network — PMC 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5998988/)
